name: Poetry Selection Inspired by Mt Madonna
description: Poems shared in community — starting with Laura's mendicant poem on not-knowing as grace. Working title; rename to taste. The two Axiom-retreat poems will be added as the author drops them in.

sections.Poem:
Let yourself become a beggar
A mendicant
An ignorant and wandering fool 
Who knows nothing

And needs everything

Let your defenses be shattered,
Let your walls come crumbling down. 

Your competency is what stands between you

And all you long for.
sections.Context: Laura shared this poem on retreat and shared it again with me. She wrote: "I keep reminding myself that not knowing and not being capable leaves room for grace."
sections.Reflection: Where in your life are you holding tight to knowing? What might soften if you let yourself not-know there?

attribution.note: Shared on retreat. Full name to be added with Laura's permission.
attribution.author: Laura

sections.Poem:
No meaning
because concepts are so limiting
if nobody meant "a" to be
the sea would still be as s-e

No point
Death eventually will consume all
And for that I thank God
So I can choose to reach beyond

No path
You're a unique contraction that connects
And I will thank God for that
And choose the path I am
sections.Context: From the Axiom book, written on retreat.
sections.Reflection: The three stanzas each begin with a "no" — no meaning, no point, no path — and each ends in a direction, a thanks, or a choice. What would it be like to hold both the no and the yes at once?

attribution.note: From the Axiom book, written on retreat.
attribution.author: PlayfulProcess

sections.Poem:
Rain drops from clouds
Clouds gather from oceans
Oceans gather from rivers
of previous rains

In a great arc drop follows drop
condensing in crystalized paths
for new, old, eternal water
to better yield to gravity

So let your tears open the path
to the nectar of love.
Love follows love, and
tears open paths for love to reign
sections.Context: From the Axiom book, written on retreat.
sections.Reflection: The poem moves from observation (how water circulates) to instruction (let your tears open the path). What in your life is a circulation you're treating as a blockage?

attribution.note: From the Axiom book, written on retreat.
attribution.author: PlayfulProcess
